EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed off the Delmarva peninsula on Monday, January 26th,  and intensified rapidly as it moved slowly northward through the 27th.  Snow spread northward across the region Monday night and became heavy on Tuesday, the 27th.   Winds became strong during the day Tuesday leading to blizzard conditions at times along and inland from the coast.  The snow persisted into Tuesday night in many areas with blowing and drifting snow.   Along the coast, large waves combined with a storm surge produced coastal flooding and splash over.  In Hampton, the Tuesday morning tide was 1.43 feet above flood levels (see graph below), inundating many streets on the bay side of town.  ||Snowfall amounts ranged from 10 to more than 30 inches across much of the southeastern part of the state.  Elsewhere, amounts were generally 6 to 14 inches with some lower amounts in the Connecticut River Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Once blizzard conditions subsided, snow and blowing snow continued into the early morning hours of the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving rapidly northeast along the East Coast on the 24th brought heavy snow to extreme southeastern New Hampshire.  Snowfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches were reported in Rockingham and eastern Hillsborough Counties.

An area of low pressure developed off the Delmarva peninsula on Monday, January 26th,  and intensified rapidly as it moved slowly northward through the 27th.  Snow spread northward across the region Monday night and became heavy on Tuesday, the 27th.   Winds became strong during the day Tuesday leading to blizzard conditions at times along and inland from the coast.  The snow persisted into Tuesday night in many areas with blowing and drifting snow.   Along the coast, large waves combined with a storm surge produced coastal flooding and splash over.  In Hampton, the Tuesday morning tide was 1.43 feet above flood levels (see graph below), inundating many streets on the bay side of town.  ||Snowfall amounts ranged from 10 to more than 30 inches across much of the southeastern part of the state.  Elsewhere, amounts were generally 6 to 14 inches with some lower amounts in the Connecticut River Valley.

Low pressure dropping southeast from Canada on the morning of the 14th intensified rapidly as it developed into two separate areas of low pressure southeast of Cape Cod. While the two lows brought a moderate to heavy snow across the southern half of the state and near blizzard conditions along the coast, the impact was much less than had been expected from the single low that was forecast to develop.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches across much of the area with up to 17 inches along the coast.

A series of low pressure areas moving along a stalled frontal boundary brought a moderate to heavy snowfall to the region over about a 60-hour period.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 6 to 15 inches with somewhat lesser amounts along the upper Connecticut River Valley.

Low pressure moving east from the Mississippi Valley on the 1st intensified off the New England coast on the 2nd before moving rapidly northeastward by the morning of the 3rd.  The storm brought heavy snow to much of the State.  Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across northern and central areas with 8 to 14 inches across southern areas.

An approaching cold front spawned thunderstorms which produced 2 to 4 inches of rain in less than 3 hours. Several road washouts occurred around Glencliff Hill Road in Benton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cold front spawned thunderstorms which produced 2 to 4 inches of rain in less than 3 hours. Several road washouts occurred around Gencliff Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado touched down briefly in the town of Warner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near Route 103 in Warner just to the east of the Exit 9 interchange on Instate 89...then moved northeast crossing Split Rock Road and then lifting off the ground just prior to reaching Kearsarge Mountain Road..  The most significant damage occurred between Split Rock and Kearsarge Mountain Roads where the tornado snapped or uprooted about 25 trees and tore a portion of the roof off a large storage building.  A 12-ft by 24-ft was found in a wooded area about 200 yards from the building.  Much of the missing roof was not found.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A woman was injured while standing on a porch when lightning struck a nearby tree.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman was struck by lightning while standing on the porch of her mobile home on Rustic Lane in Marlborough when lightning struck a tree about 10 feet away. The bolt then traveled down the tree and jumped onto the porch, hitting the woman.  Her young son who was standing next to her was uninjured.  Both were taken by ambulance to a nearby hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed near the southern New England coast on the 21st and moved northeast through the Gulf of Maine on the 22nd. Rainfall of 1 to 3 inches with this system produced minor flooding on the Suncook River at North Chichester.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding on the Suncook River at North Chichester (flood stage 7.0 ft), which crested at 7.36 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front shifted into western New England on the afternoon of June 9th and scattered convection developed ahead of the front across the Connecticut Valley during the afternoon. An isolated storm became severe and downed trees and wires in Westmoreland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and wires in Westmoreland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave swinging in from the west rapidly increased shear across the region ahead of an approaching cold front on the afternoon of June 23rd. Numerous strong to severe cells developed across New Hampshire with wind damage being the main feature of this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail near Plymouth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave swinging in from the west rapidly increased shear across the region ahead of an approaching cold front on the afternoon of June 23rd. Numerous strong to severe cells developed across New Hampshire with wind damage being the main feature of this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on Route 3 near Colebrook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave swinging in from the west rapidly increased shear across the region ahead of an approaching cold front on the afternoon of June 23rd. Numerous strong to severe cells developed across New Hampshire with wind damage being the main feature of this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree on wires in North Stratford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave swinging in from the west rapidly increased shear across the region ahead of an approaching cold front on the afternoon of June 23rd. Numerous strong to severe cells developed across New Hampshire with wind damage being the main feature of this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed large branches and power lines in Berlin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure developed between an area of low pressure moving east from the Great Lakes and a developing low off the coast.  Moderate to heavy snow developed along the trough as it moved east.  Snowfall amounts were generally in the 2 to 5 inch range with 6 to 11 inches across portions of northern Carroll and southern Coos Counties.

A trough of low pressure developed between an area of low pressure moving east from the Great Lakes and a developing low off the coast.  Moderate to heavy snow developed along the trough as it moved east.  Snowfall amounts were generally in the 2 to 5 inch range with 6 to 11 inches across portions of northern Carroll and southern Coos Counties.

A pre-frontal trough ahead of an approaching cold front was  the focus for severe convection on the afternoon of August 3rd. Moderate CAPES and low level moisture combined with increasing mid level flow to produced numerous severe cells most of which produced wind damage. One cell in northern Coos County produced several inches of rain in less than 1 hour. The Ammonoosuc River rapidly overflowed its banks and flooded portions of the Bretton Woods Golf Course.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced several inches of rain in less than 1 hour resulting in flash flooding on the Ammonoosuc River in portions of Bretton Woods Golf Course at the Mount Washington Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough was moving through a high CAPE environment on the afternoon of August 15th. Thunderstorms dropped 2 to 4 inches of rain in just a few hours causing flash flooding in Keene. Water street, Main Street and Valley Street as well as several other roads were closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped 2 to 4 inches of rain in just a few hours closing several area roads in Keene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced 2 to 4 inches of rain in a short period of time resulting in many road washouts in Hillsborough County including the towns of Bennington, Deering and Francestown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced 2 to 4 inches of rain in a short period of time in Francestown. The town Highway Superintendent reported multiple road shoulder washouts and damage to several dirt roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced 2 to 4 inches of rain in a short period of time resulting in many road washouts in Hillsborough County including the towns of Bennington, Deering and Francestown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dropped 2 to 4 inches of rain in a short time resulting in flash flooding in Bennington. Several area roads were washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developing off the North Carolina coast on the morning of the 3rd moved north to the Gulf of Maine by the morning of the 4th, then into the Canadian Maritimes by the morning of the 5th.  The storm brought a moderate to heavy snowfall across much of the State.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches across northern and central areas.  In southern New Hampshire and along the Connecticut River Valley, snowfall amounts were in the 2 to 4 inch range.

Low pressure moved to the Mid Atlantic coastline during the early morning hours of January 27th and rapidly intensified into an intense storm as it moved northeast off the New England coast. The center of the storm stalled east of Cape Cod before slowly moving off to the east on the 28th.  Relatively low astronomical tides accompanied this storm but a moderate storm surge combined with building waves to produce flooding, splash-over and erosion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense ocean storm producing a moderate storm surge and rapidly building waves caused flooding, splash-over and erosion along the New Hampshire seacoast. In Hampton New Hampshire, emergency management officials reported 18 to 24 inches of water covering Brown Avenue in the Back Bay area. The end of High Street, Kings Highway and Ashworth Street, including side street sections H to K were flooded. Significant splash-over occurred along Ocean Boulevard with minor splash-over along the town line with Rye.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developing off the North Carolina coast on the morning of the 3rd moved north to the Gulf of Maine by the morning of the 4th, then into the Canadian Maritimes by the morning of the 5th.  The storm brought a moderate to heavy snowfall across much of the State.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches across northern and central areas.  In southern New Hampshire and along the Connecticut River Valley, snowfall amounts were in the 2 to 4 inch range.

An area of low pressure moving east from the Great Lakes and a second low dropping southeast from Canada on the 29th merged over the Gulf of Maine on the 30th, then intensified rapidly.  The storms brought heavy snow to Coos County with 4 to 8 inches reported.

An area of low pressure moving rapidly northeast along the East Coast on the 24th brought heavy snow to extreme southeastern New Hampshire.  Snowfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches were reported in Rockingham and eastern Hillsborough Counties.

Low pressure moved to the New Jersey coast on the 14th of February and rapidly intensified as it moved through the Gulf of Maine early on the 15th. Storm force northerly winds over the coastal waters allowed for near shore waves to build to nearly 20 feet off Rockingham County. The Hampton tide gage reached it flood stage of eleven feet. This led to flooding of side streets in the Back Bay area, causing minor water damage of local homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense ocean storm producing storm force winds resulted in 20 foot waves along the Rockingham County coast. The Hampton tide gage reached its flood stage of 11 feet resulting in flooding of Brown Avenue and other adjacent side streets in the Back Bay area, causing some minor water damage to local homes.

An intensifying low pressure system passed well to the west of New England and into Canada during a period of extremely high astronomical tides. Ahead of this system, gale force southerly winds shifted to the southwest and diminished prior to high tide which led to only minor coastal flooding across the seacoast of New Hampshire. In Hampton, the astronomical tide of 10.7 feet was accompanied by a storm surge of 0.8 feet to lead to a 11.5 storm tide which was a half foot over the flood stage of 11.0 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An astronomically high tide of 10.7 feet combined with a storm surge of 0.8 feet to produce a storm tide of 11.5 feet in Hampton (flood stage 11.0ft). Minor flooding occurred along Brown and Island avenues near the time of high tide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of low pressure areas moving along a stalled frontal boundary brought a moderate to heavy snowfall to the region over about a 60-hour period.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 6 to 15 inches with somewhat lesser amounts along the upper Connecticut River Valley.

Low pressure rapidly intensified on the 20th of April over the Great Lakes as a secondary area of low pressure developed along the New England coastline. Gale force easterly winds over the coastal waters allowed for nearshore  waves to build to nearly 15 feet. High astronomical tides coincided with a storm surge of around a foot to produce coastal flooding and minor to moderate splash-over and beach erosion near the time of high tide. The tide was above flood stage for nearly an hour, causing minor flooding at the east end of High Street in Hampton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure produced gale force easterly winds which combined with high astronomical tides to produce minor coastal flooding at the east end of High Street in Hampton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High temperatures in the 60s and 70s resulted in rapid snow melt causing minor flooding on the Connecticut River at Dalton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid snow melt caused minor flooding on the Connecticut River at Dalton (flood stage 17.0 ft), which crested at 17.59 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree on Route 135 in Lancaster.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough ahead of an approaching cold front was  the focus for severe convection on the afternoon of August 3rd. Moderate CAPES and low-level moisture combined with increasing mid-level flow to produced numerous severe cells most of which produced wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds from a severe thunderstorm toppled and snapped trees along the Connecticut River and caused a circus tent to collapse at the Lancaster Fairgrounds.  About 100 people were under the tent at the time it collapsed.  A 41-year-old man and his 6-year-old daughter were killed in the incident.  Another 32 people were reported to have been treated at nearby hospitals.  Winds were estimated to have gusted up to 75 mph in the hardest hit area along the Connecticut River where most of the tree damage occurred.  No structural damage occurred to any of the permanent buildings at the fairgrounds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Weare.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked northeast along a stalled frontal boundary along the coast. Gale force southeasterly winds combined with the highest astronomical tides of the month to produce coastal flooding across the southwest coast of Maine. Nearshore waves built to eight feet off the coast during high tide and led to splash-over in Rockingham County. Brown and Island Avenues were flooded with up to a half foot of water near the time of high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Onshore gale force winds combined with high astronomical tides to produce coastal flooding in Rockingham County. Brown and Island Avenues in Hampton were flooded with up to a half foot of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was the focus for pulse type afternoon showers and thunderstorms, some which became severe. High values of precipitable water contributed to downbursts and several reports of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on Old Settlers Road in East Alstead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very weak upper air pattern was in place over the region on the afternoon of July 13th. Slow moving thunderstorms formed in the vicinity of a weak frontal boundary resulting in heavy rain and flash flooding in northern New Hampshire. Radar estimated 3 to 4 inches of rain in less than 3 hours in southern Coos County. In Lancaster, Lost Nation Road had some washouts. In Jefferson Route 115A and Route 115 also washed out in several locations. Mill Brook overflowed in the vicinity of the intersection of Route 115 and 115A.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding on a small brook that flows through Waumbek Golf Course in Jefferson. It rapidly overflowed its banks and washed out cart paths and flooded several fairways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very weak upper air pattern was in place over the region on the afternoon of July 13th. Slow moving thunderstorms formed in the vicinity of a weak frontal boundary resulting in heavy rain and flash flooding in northern New Hampshire. In Jefferson Route 115A and Route 115 also washed out in several locations. Mill Brook overflowed in the vicinity of the intersection of Route 115 and 115A.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding when Whipple Brook rapidly rose and went over its bank flooding a number of fields. It also flowed over Lost Nation Road and washed out the shoulder of the road. A highway manager said fill was needed in several areas along Lost Nation Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the west initiated afternoon convection across Eastern New York and Western New England on the afternoon of the 19th. High instability and increasing shear helped to develop more organized supercells and lines of thunderstorms.  Large hail and damaging winds were prevalent with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail in Union.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure in the Gulf States  on the morning of the 28th moved northeast to the Great Lake by the morning of the 29th as a secondary low developed off the mid-Atlantic coast.  The secondary low moved rapidly northeast during the day on the 29th.  The combination of the two systems brought moderate to heavy snow across the State.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 4 inches in the southwest to 6 to 8 inches in Carroll County.

A warm and humid airmass was in place across New Hampshire on the afternoon of May 27th. Thunderstorms developed during the mid afternoon across Vermont and pushed east into New Hampshire. Wind damage and torrential downpours were the main features with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Haverhill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave and associated cold front moved into northwestern New England on the afternoon of May 28th. A warm and humid airmass was in place across the region ahead of the front. Convection began firing during the early afternoon in western New England and quickly moved into New Hampshire. Damaging winds were the main threat associated with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and wires on Martin Cook Road in Richmond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front dropping south from Canada produced afternoon convection. Stronger cells developed over the Hudson Valley during the early afternoon and moved east into central and southern New Hampshire. These storms mainly produced wind damage as they pushed into Grafton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed limbs and wires in Ashland.
